result:
A small, underappreciated miracle happens whenever you change your mind.

Not the dramatic kind where a villain turns good at the last minute, or a politician reverses a position on camera. I mean the quiet, private kind: the moment you reread an old message and realize you were wrong; the second you catch yourself rehearsing an argument and suddenly see the other person’s point; the day you admit, without ceremony, that the thing you used to want no longer fits.

Changing your mind is awkward because it threatens the story you tell about yourself. We like continuity. We like being the same person from one week to the next, even if the “same person” is clearly improvising. A reversal can feel like a loss of status, a crack in the armor. It can also feel like betrayal—of your past self, of your team, of the version of you that was certain.

But certainty is often just speed. It’s the mind’s preference for a quick conclusion over the slow work of staying unsure. And the world rewards speed: hot takes, confident answers, decisive leaders. There’s no trophy for saying, “I don’t know yet, but I’m paying attention.”

Still, the best minds—whether in science, craft, or ordinary living—treat beliefs as tools rather than heirlooms. A tool should be replaced when it stops working. Keeping a broken tool because it belonged to your grandfather doesn’t build the table faster; it just makes you feel loyal while you struggle.

The hard part is noticing when a belief has stopped working. Often it doesn’t shatter; it frays. It becomes a sentence you repeat without feeling. It becomes a rule you enforce but can’t justify. It becomes a stance you defend more out of habit than conviction. Sometimes the first clue is emotional: defensiveness that arrives too quickly, or contempt that seems to do more work than evidence. If you find yourself needing to caricature the opposing view to beat it, there’s a good chance the belief is wobbling.

There’s also a gentler kind of mind-change: not surrender, but refinement. You don’t abandon the principle; you upgrade it. You stop insisting that “discipline is everything” and start noticing the machinery beneath discipline—sleep, environment, shame, support. You don’t discard “be kind” so much as you learn that kindness without boundaries becomes a slow leak. You don’t reject ambition; you learn to ask who you’re trying to impress and why.

What makes this difficult is that mind-changing is social. Ideas don’t live in your head alone; they have friends. They have communities, rituals, vocabulary, enemies. Leaving an idea can feel like leaving people. That’s why many of us hold onto beliefs long after they’ve stopped being true for us: not because we’re convinced, but because we’re connected.

So maybe the real skill isn’t “being right.” Maybe it’s building a life where you can afford to be wrong. A life where your identity isn’t one brittle thesis statement. A life with relationships sturdy enough to survive revision. A life where curiosity is not a phase but a posture.

If you want a practical test, try this: name one thing you believed five years ago that you no longer believe. If you can’t think of anything, it might not mean you’ve achieved wisdom; it might mean you’ve stopped updating. If you can think of something, take a moment to respect the person who did the updating—you. That change probably cost you something: pride, simplicity, belonging, the comfort of a clean narrative. But it also bought you something: a better map.

And better maps matter more than consistent stories. The world doesn’t reward coherence nearly as much as it punishes stubbornness.

There’s a particular relief in saying, “I used to think X, but now I think Y.” It’s the relief of movement. It’s proof you’re still in conversation with reality, not just performing yourself.
